refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting packet loss quality criteria. The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation (communication integrity) as the core functional requirement, removing the implicit 'support teleoperation' clause which is a higher-level system capability. The phrase 'communication quality meeting the customer-defined packet loss limit' is replaced with 'maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits' to emphasize the functional outcome (integrity preservation) rather than a design parameter (packet loss metric). This keeps the FR in the problem domain while remaining solution-neutral regarding network architecture, protocols, or redundancy mechanisms.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials under agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles) and measure end-to-end packet loss across representative operational scenarios. Verify that measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%). Log packet loss statistics and correlate with operator control responsiveness and safety-critical command delivery.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall enable bidirectional command transmission from tele-operator to remote equipment during teleoperation sessions.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall enable bidirectional telemetry transmission from remote equipment to tele-operator during teleoperation sessions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication loss or degradation events that exceed customer-defined packet loss thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time network quality metrics (loss rate, latency variance) to maintain command/feedback integrity within customer-defined thresholds.
DP2: Establish a dual-channel communication architecture with independent network paths and automatic failover logic to ensure continuous teleoperation control availability.

DP3: Deploy application-level forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy ratios, tuned to customer network conditions during commissioning, to absorb packet loss without requiring retransmission.

DP4: Implement a prioritized message queue with quality-of-service (QoS) differentiation, ensuring critical control commands are transmitted with higher reliability guarantees than non-critical telemetry.
DP5: Establish a closed-loop acknowledgment and timeout-driven selective repeat protocol at the application layer, with adaptive retry windows calibrated to measured round-trip latency.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP4 (Composite): Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time network quality metrics, combined with prioritized message queuing and QoS differentiation. Critical teleoperation commands (e.g., emergency stop, motion control) receive higher redundancy and acknowledgment guarantees; non-critical telemetry (e.g., sensor readings) use lighter-weight encoding. Redundancy ratios and retry thresholds are configurable and tuned during commissioning to customer network conditions.

potential_sources: DP1 and DP4 are logically orthogonal: DP1 addresses *how* to recover lost packets (redundancy + retransmission strategy); DP4 addresses *which* packets get priority treatment. They compose cleanly without circular dependencies.

Risk of tight coupling to network measurement infrastructure: DP1 requires real-time loss/latency metrics. Mitigation: define a clean abstraction (network quality observer interface) to decouple the redundancy logic from specific measurement implementations.

DP4 introduces potential coupling between message classification logic and the teleoperation control domain. Mitigation: use a declarative QoS policy (e.g., message type → priority mapping) rather than embedding priority decisions in control logic.
coupling_check.notes: Composite DP avoids over-specification of a single mechanism (e.g., FEC alone cannot adapt; dual-channel adds cost without necessity for most customer scenarios). The selected approach is modular: redundancy strategy, QoS policy, and network monitoring can evolve independently.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer network conditions are measurable and stable during agreed operational windows. RISK: If network conditions are highly volatile or unmeasurable, adaptive tuning may fail. Mitigation: require baseline network characterization as part of commissioning.

ASSUMPTION: Packet loss is the dominant failure mode; latency and jitter are secondary. RISK: If latency exceeds acceptable control loop timing, redundancy alone will not restore safety. Mitigation: establish latency acceptance criteria alongside packet loss targets.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation commands and feedback can be logically partitioned into priority tiers. RISK: Misclassification of message priority could degrade safety-critical feedback. Mitigation: define priority mapping in safety plan; validate during hazard analysis.

ASSUMPTION: Application-layer redundancy is acceptable (no requirement for transparent network-layer solutions). RISK: If customer infrastructure mandates specific network protocols, DP may require adaptation. Mitigation: confirm network stack constraints early.

ASSUMPTION: Commissioning includes tuning of redundancy ratios and retry windows. RISK: If tuning is skipped or performed incorrectly, packet loss target may not be met. Mitigation: provide automated tuning tools and acceptance test procedures.

ASSUMPTION: The 1% packet loss target is achievable with the selected DP under agreed conditions. RISK: If underlying network is fundamentally unreliable (>5% loss), no application-layer strategy will suffice. Mitigation: establish network quality baseline as a precondition for deployment.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity by ensuring that critical control commands and safety-critical feedback are delivered with ≤1% effective packet loss (or customer-defined target) under agreed network conditions, through adaptive redundancy and prioritized transmission. Non-critical telemetry may tolerate higher loss rates per customer policy. Commissioning shall include network baseline characterization and tuning of redundancy parameters to customer environment. Acceptance testing shall validate packet loss performance under simulated network conditions matching the agreed operational envelope.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Adaptive packet redundancy, selective retransmission, prioritized message queuing, QoS differentiation, and network quality metric-driven configuration for teleoperation command and telemetry transport.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives high-priority teleoperation commands (emergency stop, motion control) that require adaptive redundancy and acknowledgment guarantees.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs sensor telemetry with lighter-weight encoding and lower redundancy ratios for non-critical data.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Interfaces with physical/logical network transport; receives real-time network quality metrics and adapts redundancy/retry thresholds accordingly.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Escalates critical safety events (e.g., retransmission failures on emergency commands) to Safety Services for intervention.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: adaptive redundancy and QoS differentiation are core teleoperation concerns. Command prioritization (emergency stop, motion control) is native to this domain. Network quality metrics and configurable tuning align with teleoperation's need to adapt to variable network conditions. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert escalation requires outbound coupling to Safety Services, but this is a thin, well-defined interface.

Weak fit: Safety Services owns safety logic and alerts, not transport-layer QoS. Placing adaptive redundancy here would require Safety Services to own network transport concerns, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion: would need to couple deeply to Network Link and Telemetry, and would duplicate logic across Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Poor fit: Vehicle owns vehicle state, actuation, and sensor integration, not teleoperation transport. Placing adaptive redundancy and QoS here would scatter teleoperation concerns across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules. High diffusion: would require Vehicle to manage network metrics, message queuing, and command prioritization—all teleoperation-specific.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not a functional module. Placing DP1+DP4 here would bypass architectural layering and prevent reuse of adaptive redundancy logic in other subsystems. High diffusion: would require NGHE Solution to own low-level transport concerns, violating abstraction hierarchy.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of adaptive packet redundancy, selective retransmission, and QoS differentiation because: (1) teleoperation commands are the primary payload requiring high reliability and low latency; (2) command prioritization (emergency stop vs. motion control) is intrinsic to teleoperation semantics; (3) network quality adaptation is a teleoperation-specific concern driven by variable field conditions; (4) configurable redundancy tuning during commissioning is a teleoperation commissioning activity. The module maintains cohesion by centralizing all teleoperation transport logic and achieves low coupling through well-defined interfaces (Tasking in, Telemetry out, Network Link inout, SafetyAlert out).
containment_risks: Risk: Network Link interface may not expose sufficient real-time quality metrics (packet loss, latency, jitter) for adaptive tuning. Mitigation: ensure Network Link contract includes metric streaming or query API.

Risk: Configurable redundancy ratios and retry thresholds may require runtime parameter management (e.g., commissioning tool integration). Mitigation: define a configuration interface or parameter store accessible to Teleoperation during commissioning.

Risk: SafetyAlert escalation for retransmission failures on critical commands may require bidirectional feedback (e.g., Safety Services commanding Teleoperation to increase redundancy). Mitigation: clarify SafetyAlert contract to support advisory feedback if needed.

Risk: Telemetry encoding optimization (lighter-weight for non-critical data) may require knowledge of sensor data semantics. Mitigation: ensure Telemetry interface provides metadata (criticality flags) to guide encoding decisions.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% under agreed network conditions) is measurable and testable. Teleoperation module can be instrumented to log redundancy ratios, retransmission counts, and end-to-end packet loss during commissioning and field operation. Network quality metrics (packet loss, latency, jitter) are observable from Network Link interface. Configurable tuning parameters can be captured and validated against customer network profiles.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR is allocated to Teleoperation module with well-defined input/output interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert). Command prioritization semantics (emergency stop vs. motion control) are explicit. Adaptive behavior is driven by real-time network quality metrics, making the allocation traceable and testable.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 + DP4 composite directly addresses FR requirements. Adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission are the primary mechanisms to maintain communication integrity under packet loss. Prioritized message queuing and QoS differentiation ensure critical teleoperation commands (emergency stop, motion control) receive higher reliability guarantees. Configurable tuning to customer network conditions aligns with 'agreed network conditions' in FR. DP is solution-complete and logically sound.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Network Link interface contract must guarantee real-time streaming or query API for packet loss, latency, and jitter metrics. If metrics are unavailable or delayed, adaptive tuning logic in Teleoperation cannot respond to network degradation. Mitigation: validate Network Link interface specification includes metric SLA (e.g., metric update frequency ≤ 100ms).

Risk: Configurable redundancy ratios and retry thresholds require runtime parameter management during commissioning. If parameter storage and update mechanism are not owned by Teleoperation or exposed via a clear configuration interface, tuning logic will diffuse across Teleoperation, commissioning tools, and parameter stores. Mitigation: define a Configuration or Commissioning interface that Teleoperation owns or consumes, with clear ownership of parameter lifecycle.

Risk: SafetyAlert escalation for retransmission failures on critical commands creates a thin but critical outbound coupling. If Safety Services responds with advisory feedback (e.g., 'increase redundancy'), bidirectional coupling may emerge. Mitigation: clarify SafetyAlert contract as unidirectional event stream; if bidirectional feedback is needed, introduce a separate SafetyAdvisory inbound interface.

Risk: Telemetry encoding optimization (lighter-weight for non-critical data) requires knowledge of sensor data criticality. If Telemetry interface does not provide metadata flags (e.g., 'critical', 'non-critical'), Teleoperation must infer criticality from sensor type or hardcode rules, leading to tight coupling and maintenance burden. Mitigation: ensure Telemetry interface includes criticality metadata in message headers or schema.

Risk: Command prioritization logic (emergency stop > motion control > other) may be duplicated if Tasking interface does not explicitly tag commands with priority levels. Mitigation: require Tasking interface to include priority field; validate that Teleoperation does not re-derive priority from command type.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Teleoperation module is the natural owner of adaptive redundancy and QoS differentiation because: (1) teleoperation commands are the primary payload requiring high reliability; (2) command prioritization is intrinsic to teleoperation semantics; (3) network quality adaptation is teleoperation-specific; (4) configurable tuning is a teleoperation commissioning activity. Module achieves cohesion by centralizing all teleoperation transport logic. Coupling is low and well-defined through four interfaces. Minor diffusion risk is SafetyAlert escalation, but this is a thin, unidirectional event interface and does not violate separation of concerns. No evidence of logic scatter across Vehicle, Safety Services, or NGHE Solution top-level.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Validate Network Link interface contract includes real-time metric streaming (packet loss, latency, jitter) with update frequency SLA. If not, add a NetworkMetrics query or subscription API to Network Link.

Revision 2: Define a Configuration or Commissioning interface owned by Teleoperation (or a dedicated Configuration module) to manage redundancy ratios, retry thresholds, and priority mappings. Ensure parameter lifecycle (read, write, validate, persist) is clear and does not diffuse across modules.

Revision 3: Clarify SafetyAlert interface as unidirectional event stream (Teleoperation -> Safety Services). If bidirectional feedback is required, introduce a separate SafetyAdvisory inbound interface with explicit contract.

Revision 4: Require Telemetry interface to include criticality metadata (e.g., 'critical', 'non-critical', or numeric priority) in message headers or schema. Validate that Teleoperation uses this metadata to drive encoding decisions, not hardcoded rules.

Revision 5: Require Tasking interface to include explicit priority field (e.g., 'EMERGENCY_STOP', 'MOTION_CONTROL', 'OTHER') in command messages. Validate that Teleoperation does not re-derive priority from command type or payload inspection.

Revision 6: Add acceptance test plan that validates packet loss ≤ 1% under customer-defined network conditions (e.g., simulated packet loss, latency, jitter). Include test cases for: (a) emergency stop commands under worst-case network conditions, (b) motion control commands with adaptive redundancy, (c) telemetry with lighter-weight encoding, (d) commissioning-time tuning of redundancy ratios.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence, and containment is excellent (0.92 score). However, the DP-IM gate identifies six concrete diffusion risks that must be resolved before decomposition proceeds. These risks are not showstoppers but represent interface contracts and ownership boundaries that are currently underspecified. Revising the current level (DP and IM specifications) to address these risks will eliminate ambiguity and prevent logic scatter during implementation. Once revisions are incorporated, the FR is sufficiently mature to either stop (if no further decomposition is needed) or proceed to next-level sub-FRs with high confidence. Given the safety criticality (high) and the adaptive/configurable nature of the DP, stopping without sub-FRs is premature; revisions first, then reassess.

revise.why: Six recommended revisions target interface contracts and module ownership boundaries that must be clarified before implementation begins: (1) Network Link interface must guarantee real-time metric streaming with SLA; (2) Configuration/Commissioning interface must own parameter lifecycle; (3) SafetyAlert must be explicitly unidirectional; (4) Telemetry interface must include criticality metadata; (5) Tasking interface must include explicit priority field; (6) Acceptance test plan must validate packet loss ≤ 1% under customer network conditions. These revisions strengthen the DP (adaptive redundancy logic is clearer with explicit inputs) and IM (module boundaries are sharper with owned interfaces). Revisions do not change the core FR or DP concept but eliminate ambiguity in how the DP is realized and integrated.

assumptions: Network Link interface can provide real-time packet loss, latency, and jitter metrics with update frequency ≤ 100ms.
Teleoperation module has authority to configure redundancy ratios and retry thresholds during commissioning.
SafetyAlert interface is unidirectional (Teleoperation -> Safety Services); no bidirectional feedback is required for nominal operation.
Telemetry interface can be extended to include criticality metadata without breaking existing sensor integrations.
Tasking interface can be extended to include explicit priority field without breaking existing command sources.
Customer network conditions (packet loss, latency, jitter) can be profiled and agreed upon during commissioning.
Acceptance testing can simulate customer network conditions in a controlled environment (lab or field trial).
open_risks: Risk: Network Link interface may not support real-time metric streaming; if metrics are polled or delayed, adaptive tuning logic may not respond quickly enough to network degradation. Mitigation: validate Network Link SLA during interface design review.

Risk: Parameter lifecycle management may diffuse across Teleoperation, commissioning tools, and persistent storage if Configuration interface is not clearly owned. Mitigation: assign explicit ownership and define read/write/validate/persist semantics.

Risk: SafetyAlert escalation may evolve into bidirectional coupling if Safety Services needs to advise Teleoperation on redundancy levels. Mitigation: clarify contract as unidirectional; introduce separate SafetyAdvisory interface if feedback is needed.

Risk: Telemetry criticality metadata may not be available from all sensor sources; Teleoperation may fall back to hardcoded rules, creating maintenance burden. Mitigation: require all Telemetry sources to provide criticality metadata; define fallback behavior explicitly.

Risk: Tasking priority field may not be populated by all command sources; Teleoperation may re-derive priority from command type, duplicating logic. Mitigation: require all Tasking sources to populate priority field; validate in acceptance tests.

Risk: Acceptance testing under customer network conditions may reveal that packet loss ≤ 1% is not achievable with current redundancy strategy; DP may need to be revised (e.g., increase redundancy ratios, add forward error correction). Mitigation: conduct early prototyping and network simulation to validate DP feasibility.
evidence_needed: Network Link interface specification with metric streaming SLA (update frequency, latency, accuracy).
Configuration or Commissioning interface specification with parameter lifecycle semantics (read, write, validate, persist, rollback).
SafetyAlert interface contract clarifying unidirectionality and escalation semantics; if bidirectional feedback is needed, SafetyAdvisory interface specification.
Telemetry interface schema or metadata specification including criticality field (enum or numeric priority).
Tasking interface schema or specification including priority field (enum: EMERGENCY_STOP, MOTION_CONTROL, OTHER, or similar).

Acceptance test plan with test cases for: (a) emergency stop under worst-case network conditions, (b) motion control with adaptive redundancy, (c) telemetry encoding optimization, (d) commissioning-time tuning validation.
Network simulation or field trial results demonstrating packet loss ≤ 1% under agreed customer network conditions with current DP (adaptive redundancy + selective retransmission).
Traceability matrix linking FR acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1%) to DP mechanisms (redundancy ratios, retry thresholds) and IM test cases.
